title: Mobility Edge for Lévy Matrices
speaker: Amol Aggarwal
abstract: Lévy matrices are symmetric random matrices whose entries are in the domain of attraction of an \alpha stable law. For \alpha < 1, it had been predicted that these matrices exhibit an Anderson transition, also called a mobility edge, a point in the spectrum where eigenvector behavior sharply transitions from delocalized to localized. In this talk, we describe recent results that establish the existence and also explicitly compute the location of this mobility edge for Lévy matrices. This is based on joint work with Charles Bordenave and Patrick Lopatto.

proof_stages.label: Lévy matrix setup and global law
proof_stages.summary: The talk contrasts finite-variance Wigner universality with heavy-tailed entries, defines the n^{-1/alpha}-scaled Lévy matrix, and recalls its non-semicircular global spectral law rho_alpha.

proof_stages.label: Mobility-edge phase diagram
proof_stages.summary: Physics predictions and previous partial results are organized into a phase diagram: for alpha greater than one all finite-energy statistics should be Wigner-like, while for alpha less than one a central delocalized region should be separated from localized spectral tails.

proof_stages.label: Resolvent formulation of localization
proof_stages.summary: Localization and delocalization are formalized by the limiting imaginary part of a diagonal resolvent entry, and this is related to inverse participation ratios of eigenvectors.

proof_stages.label: Explicit threshold and main theorem
proof_stages.summary: A function lambda(e) is defined from stable-law fixed-point quantities, and the main theorem identifies delocalization where lambda(e)>1 and localization beyond the last such point, with uniqueness and asymptotics of the mobility edge near alpha=0 and alpha=1.

proof_stages.label: Poisson-weighted infinite-tree reduction
proof_stages.summary: After thresholding large entries, local neighborhoods of the complete weighted graph converge to an infinite weighted tree, and diagonal resolvents of the Lévy matrix converge to those of the corresponding tree operator.

proof_stages.label: Fractional-moment criterion for tree resolvents
proof_stages.summary: The exponential growth rate phi(s,z) of summed fractional moments of off-diagonal tree resolvents is introduced, with positivity as s approaches one implying delocalization and uniform negativity implying localization.

proof_stages.label: Transfer-operator computation and closure
proof_stages.summary: Tree expansion and Schur-complement identities reduce resolvent growth to iterates of a transfer operator whose top eigenvalue yields the explicit lambda(e), and a non-circular contradiction argument together with deterministic lambda analysis closes the theorem.

examples.name: GOE as a Wigner-matrix benchmark
examples.note: The Gaussian Orthogonal Ensemble is offered as the standard concrete finite-variance Wigner model against which Lévy-matrix behavior and GOE spacing limits are compared.
examples.engagement_reason: GOE is used as the Gaussian/Wigner benchmark for semicircle law and level-spacing universality, but no computation is carried out on a GOE matrix.

examples.name: Huge matrix entry and sparse eigenvector heuristic
examples.note: A sparse vector aligned with an exceptionally large heavy-tailed entry is used to show why extreme eigenvectors should become localized.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker reasons through a huge-entry scenario, explaining how a coordinate-supported vector becomes an approximate eigenvector with eigenvalue given by the large entry.

passes.reason: A huge entry/largest-entry heuristic with an almost coordinate eigenvector is an extreme sparse localization mechanism, not an adversarial counterexample.

examples.name: Random operator on a tree graph
examples.note: Tree random operators illustrate mobility-edge criteria and explicitly motivate the later fractional-moment phi criterion used for Lévy matrices.
examples.engagement_reason: The talk constructs and analyzes an infinite weighted tree operator, assigns Poisson edge weights, studies its resolvent entries, and formulates fractional-moment criteria on it.

examples.name: Flat vector and coordinate vector tests for inverse participation
examples.note: The flat vector gives bounded inverse participation while the coordinate vector gives divergence, clarifying how the resolvent criterion reflects eigenvector spread.
examples.engagement_reason: The flat vector and coordinate vector are plugged into the inverse-participation heuristic to see q_I equals one for the flat case and diverges for the coordinate case.

passes.reason: The flat vector and coordinate vector are extremal tests for participation, with the coordinate vector giving the minimal-support degenerate case.

examples.name: Sparse-spike heavy-tail surrogate
examples.note: A question about replacing heavy tails by rare large entries leads to a comparison with an Erdős-Rényi-plus-GOE hybrid, illustrating which features of Lévy matrices are robust heuristically.
examples.engagement_reason: In response to a question, the speaker concretely interprets a rare large-entry surrogate as a tuned sparse-spike model behaving like an Erdős–Rényi adjacency matrix plus a GOE part.

passes.reason: The sparse-spike surrogate is a simplified mostly-nonspiking mixture meant to mimic heavy tails qualitatively, not to disprove the Lévy-matrix picture.
